sentences.text: Yes.
I am Anna, A-double N-A, Reich, R-E-I-C-H.
Lipschutz, L-I-P-S-C-H-U umlaut-T-Z.
The 14 of the 9, 1920.
I'm born in Krakow, in Poland.
I have one sister.
And my mother and my two aunt, we did live together.
And life wasn't-- at-- at time, it was better.
At times, it was not very good.
I did go-- I was a child when it was depression.
And from this time, I have not very nice memories.
It was a shortage on food.
And in the winter, cold winter in Poland, there was some shortage of fuel.
And-- but my mother was always very eager-- we was-- to educate us well.
And I was a good student.
So they-- all my accents was always good.
And so I went to primary school and later, to a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] commerce and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] economic and commerce school in Krakow.
We did live in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two-- three rooms with my mother and my two aunts.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was not luxurious, but comfortable in a way, as the time was such that it's hard to compare with today's comfort.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But--
I lived in a suburb of Krakow called Podgorze.
Yes.
There was-- Krakow was an area where the-- a lot of Jewish people did congregate and felt more comfortable.
Also, traditionally, they did congregate in a part of Krakow called Kazimierz.
But we did live in Podgorze, where also quite a lot of Jewish people did live, which just dispersed outside-- what I did think it was [LAUGHS] ghetto, voluntary ghetto.
In the olden time, actually, it wasn't a voluntary ghetto.
The Jewish people was-- it was a ghetto then.
But of course, later, the people was free to disperse to live wherever they did want, yes.
I did have a-- I did have a lot of Jewish and non-Jewish friends.
I had a very good non-Jewish friend, Polish girl.
And my family was traditionally Jewish, but not terribly orthodox.
I did have a Jewish education to some extent.
And my mother did always care about the education.
And definitely, we identify ourselves as Jewish, observed all the major holidays, and-- but not orthodox.
Traditional.
I went to synagogue three times in a year.
[LAUGHS]
Well, there were some synagogues.
But for the-- the High Holy Days, there was not enough accommodation, because most of the Jews, or all of them, were traditionally observing the High Holy Days.
So sometime, some people which did have a bigger-- big accommodation did let their places.
And I was-- they was usually called [? 'shtiebel.' ?] And what was it-- they was transformed for was usually two rooms-- one for men, one for women.
And that's where they pray and observe.
There was Rosh Hoshanah, and Day of the Atonement, Yom Kippur, and sometime for Passover.
Yes, I was always very keen [LAUGHS] when my mother went to synagogue, that she looks nice.
[LAUGHS] And I remember taking big pride of making her look nice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a happy time.
And we always got new clothes for such occasion.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, I associate this time with a happy time in my life, yes.
My father was a painter.
Not artist painter-- he was a commercial painter.
Houses, yes.
No, he worked for himself.
Yes, he would take jobs whatever, you know, not just-- yes.
There was a there was a feeling of-- anti-- anti-Semitic feeling towards the Jews.
But in my town, not violent.
But I remember-- actually, I remember as little girl, they did like me.
And I congregated with the non-Jewish children, and I played with them.
And I remember on one occasion, they was cross with me for some sort of reason.
And they called me, 'Jewish girl, Jewish girl.' And that was a sort of insult [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I suppose to make me unhappy about.
Alright?] ?
And did it make you unhappy?
No.
I didn't like the comment.
It didn't make me unhappy, but I hate them for it.
I think I was seven.
I myself-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I myself did not find so much-- I didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] experience on-- on myself too much of the anti-Semitic things.
I do remember the landlord was not Jewish with the house, and [LAUGHS] there wasn't such a comfort.
And a lot of the places, the homes did not have their own bathrooms or toilets.
There was a toilet which quite a few people did have to go.
And in the winter, it was very uncomfortable, because it was outside the-- And when something went wrong with the system, he always was very angry and cold.
[LAUGHS] 'Well,' he said, 'Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are still used to-- not used to the comfort.
They should--' it's very hard to translate from Polish.
He did think that the Jews should still use the toilet behind the house, [LAUGHS] you know, sort of thing.
And whenever something went wrong, he did use these terms, right?
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] otherwise, I don't think I did experience of too much anti-Semitic.
What about in the schools?
No, no.
I did-- when I was a little girl, I remember one teacher in particular.
She was in infant school, and her son was a priest.
And she loved me very much.
I was her favorite.
[LAUGHS] So those are glimpses which I remember.
If anything, it did make me more united with the people.
And it did make me realize that it's something-- I always did feel very deeply that something should be done, that we have a homeland.
And that we are not just guests to be insulted whenever they wish.
And therefore, I did join a Zionist movement as a young girl called [?
Akiva. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a great atmosphere and wonderful feeling for looking into the future.
And I did love all the songs which they did sing, because they was full of hope, where the Yiddish songs were terribly sentimental and beautiful, but very sad.
And that did make me very happy to join this movement.
And I remember on many occasions-- [SOBBING] [?
Terrible. ?] Crying with help that there is hope for Israel.
And we did have those beautiful songs, which I still remember, which were full of hope that people would go to Israel and-- [SOBBING] Stupid.
And that Israel would eventuate.
They were terribly dedicated people which did-- it was some boys which were from very well-to-do homes and girls.
And they were so dedicated to this purpose.
They did strip themselves of all the comfort.
And [SIGHS] they were the 'haluzim,' which only did sing of one thing, to go to Israel and do hard work.
There was a lot of places which was trying-- it's called 'hachsharah.' They was-- the people was trying to be prepared for a hard life.
And they really was fantastically dedicated people.
I was very--
I was very young then.
[LAUGHS] And on one occasion, I remember it was a very rainy day.
And because they was so rough, and you know, they-- and I was still sort of a little city girl.
And I came in gloves, and it was raining-- I did have umbrella.
And they all-- they all laughed, because they said, 'Don't be so silly.
You don't need all this,' you know?
And--
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, then, I went to school.
And it was-- I interrupt-- I didn't carry on with this movement for like some of those people.
Because, you see, they-- the parents [SIGHS] of the girls did not want them to harden up so much, and go to this hachsharah, which was a big hardship, actually.
It was a hardship.
They did have to work in temperature below zero, in doing all different tasks.
And-- but it was a great spirit.
They always congregated later together in the evening, and they'd sing.
And it was really fantastic.
No, they didn't.
I just-- my life did go the way that I went to school, and-- to high school, and to-- I was very absorbed in my study.
And I always cherished the movement, but I didn't carry on.
Well, it was a family involvement, great family involvement.
It was-- we were in very, very big family.
My mother did have six sisters, and there was so many cousins, and you know.
So it was always a lot going on.
No, not all of them.
Not in the same-- no.
And my grandmother did live in a different part again called [?
Ludvinov. ?] My cousins was some living in the country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but we did live close to each other, so we visited each other very often.
Yes.
And that was like secondary and tertiary sort of education.
You could go to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] university to further your study in economics.
And it was a very good education.
It was indeed in-- I consider myself to have a good education.
That I was not a fool in-- in many ways, you know.
It was quite complete, the education, like you could recognize what materials, different texture, or is this wool, or certain metals.
And you know, you haven't-- you didn't have an education in the depth of every part of, but you were very well prepared for, like, a general knowledge.
And there was literature.
There was physics.
There was geography.
Well-- [SIGHS] well, I would say I rather came out with a good general education.
And yes, I could-- with this education, I could apply myself to all different sort of things, mostly in commerce and economics.
No, it was co-educational.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was all very-- how you say it?
That's right, that's-- that's the word, right.
Yes, but there were a lot of Jewish girls as well.
Yes, it was.
No, not myself, I must say.
There were some Hebrew schools as well, but I did not attend those.
I did have some private education in Jewish religion.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It wasn't in such a great depth.
But I-- no, I must say I can't recall it.
Maybe some small incidents, but generally, I don't think I can say-- Well, there was a general feeling when the Holy-- High Holy Days was, and there was a lot of Jewish people.
And there was this general feeling of not tolerating the way the Jew did have the beard and looked different.
And, you know, it was not the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] great tolerance in the air, you know what I mean?
Did you feel different from the Hasidic Jews and--?
Well, I don't know.
I somehow always inclined to blend.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't think that it is necessary to be dressed so separate.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I have-- as I say, mainly, I was guided by tradition and the love for the Holy Days.
And the family, we did have Friday night every week.
And for Shabbat, it was always special preparation.
So we were traditional, but not orthodox, you know?
And how did you feel about the orthodox people?
Well, there were some very lovely people.
But they were, in a way, a group for themselves, you know?
It probably is today the same.
[LAUGHS] And I have nothing against them.
I ?] [? guess ?] [? because, ?] you see, there was-- they were not such a tolerant-- I did sometimes feel maybe, why don't I reserve this for just special times?
Or so it doesn't-- you know, it doesn't point the finger so much.
That's how I felt, but--
Gee, it's amazing how much I remember.
Well, I did-- I did work-- first, can I tell you?
I worked at one firm in Krakow which was a very special job.
And.. those people which could get a job in this particular firm was lucky, because they was always special gratitude.
The wages was better.
And-- and the bosses was Jewish.
The name was Alexandrowicz.
They was very, very well known, very philanthropic family in Krakow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a wholesale-- huge magazines [? right ?] through Poland of paper, paper in all sorts of ways-- bulk paper, newspaper.
And it was-- it was a very big, profitable business.
And I worked there.
And that did bring me to the brink of the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The bosses did have some orchards in Israel, I remember.
And my boss was so lovely.
There were two brothers, Sigmund and-- oh dear, I can't seem to remember the other name.
And everyday in the morning, when I opened my desk, there was a big Jaffa orange there for me, which was such a lovely feeling.
And they went to Israel, what I understand, in the beginning of the war.
They did-- I think they ran away, but I'm not sure what happened so that I couldn't follow it.
[SIGHS] I was working in the office, bookkeeping.
Part-- it was-- the bookkeeping department was very, very big.
There was-- I don't know, must be 30 people working in just this department.
And I was one part of it.
No, they was terribly generous people and terribly loving people.
I did never ask [LAUGHS] or anybody else did find the orange in their desk.
But I was always very happy to find one.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, yes.
Yes.
It was all Jewish, yes.
Well, it was interesting, because to get this job, it was almost impossible, because everybody tried.
But when I got my good result from school-- I [LAUGHS] remember I tried to look a bit more grown-up I was 17.
And I remember putting on my auntie's outfit.
And I did think, I'll just try to go there and ask for the owner to have a word with him.
And when I come there, it wasn't easy, because they was-- they did have their offices, and it wasn't easy to speak to them.
But one of the-- office people came and asked me, 'Can I help you?' And I said, 'I would like to speak to Mr.
Alexandrovicz.
And he said, 'Well, what it is about?' And I said, 'Well, should I tell you I'm trying to get a job here?' And in this instance, I remember Mr.
Alexandrovicz came out of the office.
And this man who worked there-- what was his name?
Can't remember.
He laughed, and he laughed towards-- Mr.
Alexandrovicz came closer.
And he said, 'This is a young lady who tried to get a job here.' And he looked at me and smiled.
And he said, 'Wait a minute,' and asked me to the office, in the office.
And I said, 'Yes,' and that my wish always was to work here.
And somehow, he was impressed, and I got the job.
Oh, we did have a daily contact with the bosses, because they work-- they were almost at all the time in the office-- in the business, yeah.
I think it must be two years, yes.
It was '38.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '38, yes.
And then, when the war started and the bosses did run away-- actually, I must remember-- there were also people employed, not Jewish, actually.
In the office, they was Jewish.
But there was non-Jewish-- three brothers, I remember, Kafchinski was their name, lovely people who was very involved on the high position in this business, yes.
But then, when both brothers Alexandrovicz fled, the business was taken by what was called then a 'Treuhander.' And I remember his name, a German-- Kiesel was his name.
And well, the bosses wasn't there, of course.
The business still did carry on.
treuhander was-- is a German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] word for a person who steps in as an official to take over the business.
And that was many, many Jewish firm [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was disowned, and then the German [?
Treuhanders ?] did step in.
Yes, I continued to work there until until ghetto was formed.
I remember-- it was a very strong impression, because we did think that the Polish was well-equipped.
As a young person, I did think their uniform was always spic-and-span and tidy.
And I did think, somehow, Poland is strong.
The Jews, I must also add-- the Jews was during the [?
Marshal ?] Pilsudski reign-- The Jews--
Before the war.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I think that was-- I would like to put exactly when his reign was, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a very well-known fact that during his reign, Josef Pilsudski-- the Jews did have the best time in Krakow-- well, in Poland.
And he was a very tolerant man.
The Jews really did, at this time, enjoy freedom and prosper.
And I remember when he die from-- the school took part in the funeral.
And I was very moved by it.
And this was a dying era for a lot of freedom for the Jews in Krakow.
[SIGHS] I think it was 1935.
And the things did change after that, yes.
And then, of course-- as I said, I did think Poland is strong.
I was very patriotic, in fact, [LAUGHS] and very emotional.
When they did sing the Polish national hymn, I always remember having tears in my eyes, you know?
Somehow I was--
Both, both.
Right.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, I did think Poland is strong for some reason.
And when the first German tanks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] entered Krakow, and I did see it, that was, to me, like people from the other planet.
It was so newly equipped, and so strong, and so overpowering.
And so [SIGHS] then I understood, and I felt how weak Poland was.
And the bad--
